title: Robustness and dynamic sentiment
abstract: Errors in survey expectations display waves of pessimism and optimism. This paper develops a novel theoretical framework of time-varying beliefs capturing this fact. In our model, dynamic beliefs arise endogenously due to agents‚Äô attitude towards alternative models. Decision-maker‚Äôs distorted beliefs generate countercyclical risk aversion, procyclical portfolio weights, and countercyclical equilibrium asset returns. A calibrated version of our model is shown to jointly match salient features in survey data and equity markets.

A regime-switching model between pessimism and optimismIn the previous section, while the degree of pessimism or optimism in the agent‚Äôs beliefs depends dynamically on Z1‚ąíő∑, the agent is either optimistic or pessimistic. To allow for both sentiment states to be present, we introduce a regime-switching model in which the agent changes between optimism and pessimism dynamically. The idea is that when the agent feels less pessimistic, she is more likely to switch to the optimistic state; conversely, when the agent feels less optimistic, she is more likely to switch back to the pessimistic state. In other words, the intensities of switching between states also depend on the state variable Z.To this end, let I be a process taking values in {O,P} indicating whether the agent is in the optimistic or pessimistic state. Let őĹ=inf{s‚Č•t:Is‚Č†It}, i.e., the first time when the agent switches between optimism and pessimism after time t. It equals the minimization on the right-hand side, which incorporates the discounted intertemporal utility, the Cressie-Read penalty, and the discounted bequest utility if the agent remains pessimistic until the terminal time, or the discounted optimistic utility VőĹO,c if the agent switches to optimism before the terminal time. Suppose the agent starts in the pessimistic state. Negative fundamental shocks to BB increase the value of Z1‚ąíő∑, hence, the agent becomes more pessimistic. The effects for ő∑>1 are opposite.We now turn to a long horizon with T up to 100 years in order to study intertemporal hedging and resulting horizon effects. The seminal work of Merton (1973) explains how non-myopic investors tilt their portfolio in order to hedge against future changes in the investment opportunity set. For example, if returns on a risky asset are contemporaneously negatively correlated with expected returns on that asset, it becomes less risky to hold over longer horizons, inducing investors with longer horizons to increase their holdings. Despite returns being i.i.d. in our setting, intertemporal hedging appears because of the investor‚Äôs distorted beliefs; even though expected returns are constant under the reference measure B, they are not constant under U when ő∑‚Č†1. In particular, because of time-varying sentiment, the belief distortion u reacts to return innovations. When the belief distortion is countercyclical (ő∑<1), positive return shocks reduce pessimism or strengthen optimism, which increase the perceived expected return on the risky asset. In other words, for ő∑<1, returns and expected returns under U are positively correlated, which leads to negative intertemporal hedging demands that grow with the horizon, see the right two panels of Fig. 3. Recall that for ő∑>1, positive return shocks strengthen pessimism or reduce optimism and therefore decrease the perceived expected return, so we now have a negative correlation between historic returns and expected return. This explains the positive intertemporal hedging demand for ő∑>1, which naturally grows with the horizon, see the right two panels of Fig. 3. We also observe from Fig. 3 that the relationship between ő∑ and the sentiment state is asymmetric. When the agent is pessimistic, effects are more pronounced for ő∑<1 than for ő∑>1 for the same deviation from ő∑=1, for example, ő∑=0.5 and ő∑=1.5. article.4. Estimating sentiment: We now estimate a measure of time-varying sentiment from the data. We first measure the difference between agents‚Äô subjective and objective beliefs. To measure subjective beliefs, we make use of a survey on aggregate GDP growth. Objective beliefs are calculated from a vector autoregression (VAR) from which we infer forecasts of macroeconomic variables. The difference between the subjective and objective beliefs then allows us to back out an empirical proxy for the optimal distortion in our model. Our data sample runs from January 1990 to December 2019.27Objective beliefs: To get a proxy for objective beliefs, we estimate a VAR with two lags on GDP, inflation, the Fed Funds Rate, and unemployment. Data is quarterly and available from the Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED). Forecasts from the VAR(2) then represent the objective belief.28Subjective beliefs: To measure subjective beliefs, we use the Survey of Professional Forecasters (SPF). SPF data are publicly available professional forecasts run by the Federal Reserve Bank of Philadelphia. Forecasters provide forecasts for different horizons and we focus on the one-quarter ahead forecast. We define the subjective belief as the median (consensus) one-quarter ahead forecast.Finally, the wedge is defined as the difference between the subjective and objective beliefs about future GDP growth, measuring the amount of pessimism or optimism in the overall economy. Fig. 1 plots realized output growth, subjective, objective output growth, and the resulting wedge. The figure reveals that before economic recessions, beliefs are optimistic, whereas there is a dramatic drop in GDP wedges turning to pessimism after recessions. While the rebound after the 2001 crisis has been fast, the repercussions of the 2008 Great Financial Crisis are long lasting, leading to pessimism on average. In our calibrations, we target the first three moments of the wedge distribution and the proportion of each sentiment state. The average GDP wedge is around ‚ąí0.98% with an associated standard deviation of 1.10% and a skewness of ‚ąí0.18. The wedge is negative 80% of the time during the sample period, indicating pessimism on average during this period.29Table 3. Belief wedges and gdp growth. Belief wedges and asset pricesWe can now study model-implied asset prices. Panel B of Table 4 reports the unconditional equilibrium asset pricing results produced by the calibrated model, which is disciplined by the first three moments of belief wedges and the proportion of each sentiment state, tabulated in Panel A. The calibrated model matches these properties of the belief wedge almost perfectly, except for the third moment, where the calibrated model generates considerably more negative skewness, due to the large magnitude of the negative structural shock during the 2007‚Äď2008 global financial crisis. We also report the AR(1) coefficient of wedge dynamics. Our model produces an AR(1) coefficient of 0.84, in between the empirical counterpart and the unit AR(1) coefficient of the entropy case.The equilibrium asset pricing results are primarily driven by pessimism in the calibrated model, because the data shows that wedges are mostly negative. The model performs well in generating a sizable risk premium and realistic Sharpe ratio, although both fall short of the high empirical values observed in our sample period. The quantitative success in producing excess volatility is more limited. Higher equilibrium volatility could be obtained in the model by injecting some stochastic volatility in the consumption process, following the insights of the long-run risk literature (Bansal and Yaron, 2004 and others). Instead we choose to fully isolate the quantitative effect of the Cressie-Read mechanism, in particular in terms of its ability to generate interesting dynamics despite the deliberately stylized nature of the i.i.d. Gaussian fundamentals, which are by design void of any dynamics.To assess the impact of Cressie-Read relative to entropy for unconditional asset pricing, we set ő∑=1, qO=0.02, and qP=0.014, in order to match the percentage of pessimistic periods, and use otherwise the same parameters. We see that our Cressie-Read extension is critical for matching the empirically observed moments of the belief wedges, as well as for explaining asset pricing stylized facts. When ő∑=1, the belief wedge is constant in a given regime. The dynamics are therefore solely driven by regime switching between pessimism and optimism. This is reflected in the very poor performance of the entropy case when it comes to matching the second and third moment of belief wedges in Table 4. In the case of relative entropy the model also fails to address the equity premium puzzle or the risk-free rate puzzle: we find a much higher risk-free rate of 4.16%, a very low equity risk premium of 1.34% percent, in addition to a constant return volatility equal to the volatility of dividends, and a low Sharpe Ratio of 0.1. We therefore conclude by noting that our Cressie-Read extension improves substantially on the quantitative front, in addition to generating meaningful time-variation at the business cycle frequency.We now zoom in further on the conditional asset pricing implications of our model and its ability to explain observed time variation in valuation ratios and stock returns. Fig. 5 plots the time-series of the (log) price‚Äďdividend ratio and one-year stock return, both as empirically observed and as generated by our model conditional on realizations of fundamentals. While the model-generated price‚Äďdividend ratio is less volatile than empirically observed, as is apparent from the different scales for both time-series, the model explains a substantial portion of the time-variation in the valuation ratio. The time-series correlation between empirical and model-generated price‚Äďdividend ratio is 72%. All panels center at the mean of the sentiment variable and span two standard deviations above and below of the distribution for the sentiment variable during the observation period.The state- and regime-dependence of objective and subjective expected returns are plotted in Fig. 7. The subjective expected return is lower than the objective expected return in the pessimistic state, and vice versa in the optimistic state. This is not surprising, and due to u>0 (u<0) in the pessimistic (optimistic) state. In the pessimistic state, which happens more than 80% of time, the subjective expected return is flatter than the objective version, i.e., exhibits less state-dependence. In line with intuition and asset pricing stylized facts, the objective expected return is higher in the pessimistic state than in the optimistic state. The subjective expected returns are roughly at the same level between the pessimistic state and the optimistic state. Finally, the subjective expected return is procyclical when optimistic, and countercyclical when pessimistic. The procyclicality of subjective expected returns can be interpreted as the optimistic agent extrapolating: when optimistic and when experiencing positive (negative) fundamental shocks, subjective expected returns increase (decrease).Turning to the objective and subjective risk premia in Fig. 8, we note that when pessimistic, both risk premia are countercyclical, but the state-dependence is more pronounced for the objective risk premium than for the subjective one. In the optimistic regime, both the objective and subjective risk premium exhibits substantially less cyclicality. Importantly, the gap between the objective and the subjective risk premium is reasonable and around 1% at the mean of the state space, while it spans less than 2% within two standard deviations above and below the mean of the state variable the state space, only reaching 2% in the rare scenario of the most extreme pessimism. As Hansen and Sargent (2022) and Chamberlain (2020) both point out, a central idea in robust Bayesian analysis based on classical work of Good (1952) is to judge the plausibility of a min‚Äďmax model by examining how reasonable the subjective belief U is that is supporting the equilibrium. It seems fair to conclude that even the most extreme pessimism in our calibration passes Good‚Äôs plausibility criterion. Finally, in the pessimistic regime, the jump risk premium is an order of magnitude smaller than the total risk premium, indicating that most of the risk premium comes from state-dependence induced by our Cressie-Read mechanism of dynamic sentiment, not from regime switching. When optimistic, the jump risk premium is more important and makes up most of the objective risk premium, which is not surprising because it reflects the risk of jumping from optimism to pessimism.
article.6. Conclusions: Our paper makes the following contributions. First, we propose a new model of dynamic sentiment. In the model, beliefs exhibit episodes of pessimism and optimism and are driven by past belief distortions and fundamental shocks. These dynamic patterns arise endogenously as agents fear misspecification of the benchmark model and seek robustness by considering alternative models. We generalize existing approaches to robustness in two ways. First, we replace the entropy criterion that is ubiquitous in the literature on robust control by the family of Cressie-Read divergences. Our Cressie-Read divergence measure is recursive and homothetic, leading to time-consistent preferences and tractable decision problems. This extension opens the door to endogenous and state-dependent dynamics of belief distortions, governed by the Cressie-Read parameter ő∑. In particular, the dynamics of sentiment can be procyclical or countercyclical depending on the sign of 1‚ąíő∑. Sentiment is driven by past fundamental shocks, as well as by past belief distortions, thereby generating the time-varying nature of sentiment observed empirically. Second, our model allows for optimism in addition to pessimism, and features a regime-switching mechanism where the likelihood of switching depends also on the sentiment state variable.As a second contribution, we apply our model to understand portfolio choice and general equilibrium asset pricing. We highlight the ability of the model to generate rich dynamics and to explain empirically relevant phenomena by deliberately assuming i.i.d. Gaussian fundamentals, in which case entropy produces constant portfolios, risk premia, interest rates, and volatility. In our model, endogenous sentiment gives rise to stochastic effective risk aversion. When the Cressie-Read parameter ő∑ is smaller than one, effective risk aversion is countercyclical, while the opposite happens whenever ő∑>1. In our portfolio problem, this induces intertemporal hedging and therefore both horizon- and state-dependent portfolios, despite returns being i.i.d. We calibrate our general equilibrium asset pricing model using estimates of belief distortions obtained from survey data of expectations by professional forecasters about future economic activity. We provide empirical support for key testable implications of the model regarding the relationships between fundamental shocks to the economy, belief wedge dynamics, and forecast errors, as well as conditional asset pricing.
